skill.name: aws-penetration-testing
skill.description: Comprehensive AWS penetration testing and security assessment skill for authorized red team operations, IAM enumeration, and cloud security auditing.
skill.summary: Provides techniques for authorized AWS security testing including IAM enumeration, SSRF exploitation, S3 bucket assessment, Lambda security analysis, and privilege escalation detection for red team engagements.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 287 patterns across 2 files (881 lines). Most findings are FALSE POSITIVEs because files contain Markdown documentation (not executable code). However, content includes sensitive offensive security techniques (SSRF exploitation, privilege escalation, persistence mechanisms) requiring explicit authorization warnings. Recommend: publish with prominent authorization disclaimers and user acknowledgment requirements.

high_findings.title: SSRF to AWS Metadata Endpoint Documentation
high_findings.description: Skill documents SSRF exploitation technique targeting AWS Instance Metadata Service (IMDS). While educational for authorized testing, this technique can extract IAM role credentials from compromised EC2 instances. Static analyzer correctly identified metadata endpoint patterns (169.254.169.254).

high_findings.title: Privilege Escalation Techniques
high_findings.description: Documents multiple AWS IAM privilege escalation paths including CreateAccessKey, AttachUserPolicy, and Lambda code injection. These are legitimate security testing techniques but require explicit authorization.

high_findings.title: Credential Extraction Methods
high_findings.description: Documents techniques for extracting credentials from Lambda environment variables, EBS volumes, and container metadata. Educational for defensive security but offensive in nature.

medium_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positives - External Commands
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 140 'external_commands' patterns. These are FALSE POSITIVEs - the patterns appear in Markdown code blocks (documentation), not executable scripts. Commands are educational examples for AWS CLI usage during authorized penetration testing.

medium_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positives - Cryptographic Patterns
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' patterns. These are FALSE POSITIVEs - no actual cryptographic implementations exist. Pattern matches likely triggered on documentation text mentioning encryption concepts.

medium_findings.title: Static Analysis False Positives - C2/Malware Keywords
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'C2 keywords' and 'malware type keywords'. These are FALSE POSITIVEs - terms like 'backdoor' appear in educational security context (e.g., 'Lambda Backdooring' as a technique to detect/test for). No actual malware or C2 infrastructure present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and IP Addresses
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged hardcoded URLs and IP addresses. These are FALSE POSITIVEs - URLs are AWS service endpoints (s3.amazonaws.com), documentation references (github.com), or AWS metadata IPs (169.254.169.254) documented for educational purposes.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Commands
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged reconnaissance patterns. These are standard AWS CLI commands (describe-instances, list-users) for authorized security auditing. Low risk in educational documentation context.

dangerous_patterns.title: AWS Credential Export Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation shows export commands for AWS credentials (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_SESSION_TOKEN). While standard AWS CLI usage, combined with SSRF credential extraction techniques creates risk profile.

dangerous_patterns.title: EBS Volume Mount for Credential Extraction
dangerous_patterns.description: Documents technique to attach attacker-controlled EC2 instance to target EBS volume, mount filesystem, and extract credentials. Legitimate forensics technique but also used in attacks.

dangerous_patterns.title: CloudTrail Disabling Techniques
dangerous_patterns.description: Documents methods to disable or modify CloudTrail logging. While relevant for red team simulation, this technique directly impacts audit trail integrity.

content.user_title: Perform AWS Penetration Testing and Security Assessment
content.value_statement: Organizations need to validate their AWS cloud security posture against real-world attack techniques. This skill provides authorized security teams with comprehensive methodologies for IAM enumeration, SSRF exploitation testing, S3 bucket assessment, and privilege escalation detection.
actual_capabilities: Enumerate IAM users, roles, groups, and attached policies using AWS CLI
Test SSRF vulnerabilities against AWS Instance Metadata Service (IMDSv1 and IMDSv2)
Assess S3 bucket configurations for public access and misconfigurations
Analyze Lambda functions for code injection and privilege escalation paths
Document credential exposure risks in EBS volumes and container environments
Generate security audit findings with remediation recommendations
limitations: Requires valid AWS credentials and explicit written authorization before use
Cannot execute AWS CLI commands directly - provides guidance only
Not intended for production environments without change approval process
Does not include automated scanning - manual testing methodology required

use_cases.title: Authorized Red Team Engagement
use_cases.description: Security consultants performing authorized penetration testing against client AWS environments to identify misconfigurations and privilege escalation paths before malicious actors exploit them.
use_cases.target_user: Security consultants and red team operators

use_cases.title: Cloud Security Audit
use_cases.description: Internal security teams assessing their organization's AWS security posture against known attack techniques to validate defensive controls and monitoring capabilities.

use_cases.title: Security Research and Training
use_cases.description: Security researchers and students learning AWS attack techniques in controlled lab environments (AWSGoat, CloudGoat) to improve defensive security skills.
use_cases.target_user: Security researchers and cloud security students

prompt_templates.title: Basic IAM Enumeration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me enumerate IAM permissions for the current AWS identity. I have authorized access and need to document what permissions this identity has. Start with sts get-caller-identity and show me how to list attached policies.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial reconnaissance during authorized security assessment

prompt_templates.title: S3 Bucket Security Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to assess S3 bucket configurations for public access vulnerabilities. Show me the AWS CLI commands to list buckets, check bucket policies, and identify publicly accessible objects in our authorized test environment.

prompt_templates.title: SSRF to Metadata Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: We're testing our web application for SSRF vulnerabilities that could access AWS metadata endpoints. Document the IMDSv1 and IMDSv2 techniques so we can verify our instance metadata protection controls are working.

prompt_templates.title: Privilege Escalation Path Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the IAM permissions we've enumerated and identify potential privilege escalation paths. Check for dangerous permissions like iam:CreateAccessKey, iam:AttachUserPolicy, and lambda:UpdateFunctionCode that could lead to admin access.

output_examples.input: Enumerate current IAM identity and permissions
output_examples.output:
Identity: arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:user/test-user
Attached Policies: AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess, CloudWatchLogsReadOnly
Inline Policies: None
Escalation Risk: LOW - No privilege escalation permissions detected

output_examples.input: Check S3 bucket public access configuration
output_examples.output:
Bucket: company-assets
Public Access Block: Enabled
Bucket Policy: Denies public access
ACL: Bucket-owner-enforced
Status: SECURE - No public access vectors identified

IMDS Version: IMDSv2 enabled
Token Required: Yes
Metadata Access: Protected
Status: SECURE - IMDSv2 token requirement prevents SSRF exploitation

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization documenting scope, systems, and testing window before beginning any penetration testing activities
Enable CloudTrail logging before testing begins and preserve all logs for post-engagement analysis and client reporting
Use dedicated test credentials and avoid testing against production environments without explicit change approval and rollback procedures
anti_patterns: Never test AWS resources outside the authorized scope - unauthorized access violates computer crime laws even with good intentions
Do not disable security controls (CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Security Hub) permanently - temporary bypasses must be documented and restored
Avoid leaving persistent backdoors or access mechanisms after engagement completion - all test artifacts must be removed during cleanup

faq.question: What authorization do I need before using this skill?
faq.answer: You must have written authorization from the AWS account owner documenting: specific accounts/resources in scope, testing time window, approved techniques, and emergency contact procedures. Unauthorized testing violates AWS Acceptable Use Policy and computer crime laws.

faq.question: Can this skill execute AWS commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides guidance and documentation for AWS CLI commands. You must manually execute commands in your own terminal with your own credentials. The skill cannot access your AWS account directly.

faq.question: Is it legal to test AWS services I don't own?
faq.answer: No. You may only test AWS resources you own or have explicit written authorization to test. AWS prohibits unauthorized penetration testing in their Acceptable Use Policy. Contact AWS Security for their penetration testing policy if testing AWS infrastructure.

faq.question: What tools do I need to use this skill effectively?
faq.answer: Required: AWS CLI configured with credentials, Python 3, and boto3 library. Recommended: Pacu (AWS exploitation framework), Prowler or ScoutSuite (security auditing), enumerate-iam (permission enumeration). All tools should only be used in authorized environments.

faq.question: How do I prevent GuardDuty alerts during testing?
faq.answer: GuardDuty will likely generate alerts during penetration testing. This is expected behavior. Coordinate with the security operations team to whitelist test activity or temporarily adjust alerting thresholds. Never disable GuardDuty without authorization.

faq.question: What cleanup is required after testing?
faq.answer: Remove all created resources (IAM users, access keys, Lambda functions, EC2 instances). Delete any backdoored code or persistence mechanisms. Restore modified configurations. Document all changes made for client verification. Preserve testing logs for the final report.
